title: One Nation could pick up seats from NDIS reform
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 Opinion One Nation could pick up seats from NDIS reform The Australian Financial Review John Black 29 April 2026 1080 words English AFNR 31 Copyright 2026. Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited. Elections Labor is about to learn that pushing through real structural changes to the budget creates winners and losers - and losers have longer memories than winners. The Albanese government has finally got serious about the spending side of the federal budget, starting with reforms to what Labor's Health Minister Mark Butler calls the structural flaws in the original design of the NDIS. But when we strip away the economic need to preserve the NDIS in a sustainable form, what do we see in terms of winners and losers, and what are the longer-term political implications for the Albanese government? The winners from cut-backs to the NDIS are voters in wealthy federal Labor and teal seats that contain relatively few NDIS participants and associated jobs, but which are paying the bulk of the taxes to sustain the scheme. Labor's reforms potentially take the pressure off these voters for future tax increases and offer faint hope for an income tax cut as Labor bait for the 2028 election. These 20 federal seats with per capita numbers of NDIS participants averaging 1.5 per cent have very high socio-economic status (SES). Each family holds down two secure professional jobs, yielding family incomes higher than $4000 per week. This puts them in the top 10 per cent of taxpayers, who provide more than half the income tax dollars used to fund government spending. These voters are highly mobile and often live in rental units. They are more likely to be overseas-born and of Chinese or Indian ancestry. They are young and relatively healthy, with little reliance on Commonwealth transfer payments. They tend to buy private healthcare, but draw very little in claims per member and subsidise those who do. They tend to have only one child in their late teens, attending a high-fee non-government school or university. In 2025, 13 of these 20 seats were safe Labor or moving that way, with four of the 20 won by Labor from the Greens and Liberals in 2025. Six of the seats were won by teals, including Bradfield won from the Liberals in 2025. There was one lonely Liberal seat of Berowra, likely to be won by Labor in 2028, given strong swings to Labor from high SES voters in recent elections, including in South Australia. The losers from restructuring of the NDIS are voters in federal seats with high per capita numbers of the 761,000 NDIS participants as at December 2025, and the associated 500,000 part-time income streams and 311,000 full-time equivalent jobs for disability support workers and allied health professionals, as revealed by outgoing NDIS minister Bill Shorten in early 2025. The NDIS participants themselves would be among those with three or more chronic health conditions, including asthma, lung disease, diabetes, arthritis, kidney disease or a mental health condition. They often can't afford private health insurance, but if they can, they draw heavily on benefits per member. Their families and support workers tend to be older and Australian-born, of English ancestry, with kids at government schools. Many are activist Christians. They have certificate-level qualifications and lower-than-average incomes that range widely across the second and third quartiles. Their support workers hold down insecure unskilled, semi-skilled or skilled blue-collar jobs in trades or service sectors. Participation rates in the workforce are low and total transfer payment levels are approaching 50 per cent. Due to the large numbers of both NDIS participants and their support workers in these top 20 NDIS seats, we're looking at an average of more than 7000 NDIS participants and similar numbers of support workers in average seats of about 120,000 voters. That's one in eight voters who are depending on the NDIS for part of their incomes and care, before we even look at the families of these persons. For example, at the top of the 20 NDIS seats is the Federal seat of Spence in northern Adelaide, where solid Labor voting and unionised UK migrant workers used to make Holden cars, sewing machines and vacuum cleaners. Now the seat contains 11,500 participants in the NDIS and there would be a similar number of support workers, making up one in seven voters. At the recent South Australian state election, one in three of these voters voted for One Nation and four in 10 for Labor. Of these top 20 NDIS seats, 13 are provincial city or peri-urban seats held by the ALP and under varying threat levels from One Nation. On the South Australian state vote modelling, both Hunter and Paterson would be lost to One Nation in 2028. There are six National Party seats in the top 20. On the SA voting model figures, Gippsland and Hinkler would be lost to One Nation, Cowper would be lost to a teal and Page, Mallee and Nicholls would be narrowly retained by the Nationals on preferences from One Nation or Labor. At the moment, Labor's reforms to the NDIS have been supported to varying degrees by leaders of the National Party (about bloody time - Matt Canavan), One Nation (waste, fraud and abuse - Pauline Hanson) and the Liberals (we will work with them - Angus Taylor), but how long will this display of economic virtue survive sustained pressure from vested interests between now and 2028? This pressure will emerge via the social media exploitation of severely disabled NDIS beneficiaries by the shonks who have been ripping off the system in more recent times and by political opportunists chasing their votes. They will be cheered on across the political spectrum by the Greens on the political left, sniffing out a conspiracy theory somewhere, to the Coalition state government in Queensland, which so loves to shift their health costs to the feds that there were reports in medical circles a few years back that one big Brisbane state hospital was shuffling patients out of their beds in their pyjamas, their IV drips still in their arms, to get scans at the federally funded GP practice across the road. The Albanese government is about to learn that pushing through real structural changes to the budget creates winners and losers, and losers have longer memories than the winners. John Black is a former Labor senator for Queensland. He is executive chairman of profiling company Australian Development Strategies, and of Education Geographics. summary: The Albanese government is implementing structural reforms to the NDIS, which may benefit wealthy Labor and teal seats while disadvantaging areas with high NDIS participation. The political implications could see One Nation gaining support in regions affected by these reforms. The changes highlight the tension between fiscal sustainability and the needs of vulnerable populations reliant on the NDIS.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: General Insurance
key_points: Labor's NDIS reforms may create winners and losers among voters.
Wealthy areas with fewer NDIS participants could see tax relief, while high-participation areas may face challenges.
One Nation could gain traction in regions adversely affected by the reforms.
relevance: Understanding the political and economic implications of NDIS reforms is crucial for actuaries and financial professionals involved in policy analysis and risk assessment.
